does: This paper develops an interpretable out-of-distribution detection method for deep neural networks in medical imaging by measuring how stable model predictions remain under semantic perturbations. The approach uses sparse autoencoders to learn class-specific concept vectors that decompose dense neural representations into sparse, interpretable semantic components. At test time, the method perturbs intermediate representations using concept vectors corresponding to the model's predicted class and measures the resulting stability of class logits. The core claim is that in-distribution samples will show low sensitivity to these perturbations because their representations align with learned class-specific semantic features, while out-of-distribution samples will exhibit greater instability.
connects: This work builds on the interpretability literature that seeks to understand what neural networks learn through concept-based methods and sparse decompositions, combining this with the established OOD detection problem. Recent work has used sparse autoencoders to extract interpretable features from neural networks, while the broader OOD detection field has explored confidence calibration, likelihood estimation, and input perturbation approaches. The paper responds to the limitation that most existing OOD methods rely on opaque internal signals like softmax confidence or intermediate activations whose semantic meaning remains unclear, particularly problematic for medical applications. It connects representation stability under perturbation to distributional shift, extending ideas from adversarial robustness that examine how networks respond to input modifications.
enables: This framework enables future work on truly interpretable safety mechanisms for deep learning in clinical settings by grounding OOD detection in human-understandable semantic concepts rather than abstract statistics. It points toward methods that could simultaneously detect dangerous distribution shifts and explain why a prediction is suspect through explicit concept perturbation analysis. The approach could inspire follow-up research combining sparse decomposition with other OOD techniques, extending the method to other domains beyond medical imaging, or developing concept-based interventions for model retraining. It also opens questions about which concept-perturbation strategies most reliably distinguish in from out-of-distribution data, and whether learned concepts could be validated against expert medical knowledge to further increase clinical trustworthiness.
